narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 36, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the lowest 10th percentile. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 94.6% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to students who typically pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, well below the average of around the 55th percentile, signaling weaker performance in later undergraduate years that strongly correlates with bar struggles for many students. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 10% versus the typical mid-50s percentile, reinforcing concerns about early academic consistency. Your LSAT score of 36, while solid, pulled slightly in the positive direction since it's close to the dataset average of 36.7, helping offset some risk but not enough to counter the academic decile impacts. Overall, your grades placed you below typical admitted students, where stronger decile rankings often predict bar passage, unlike your situation. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially as someone from an upper-middle background pursuing full-time study. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view, and many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting schools with holistic reviews that value diverse backgrounds like yours. Keep pushing—you've got the determination to rewrite this story.
